name: Contextual priors
description: Cognitive science framework that prior context and learned expectations shape what attention, perception, and retrieval prioritize from incoming information; the through-line connecting Barbara's dissertation on context-guided visual attention to her contemporary work on RAG and grounded AI.

name: David Marr's levels of analysis
description: Three-level analytical framework for any information-processing system — computational (what and why), algorithmic (how), implementation (physical realization) — that Barbara uses to keep purpose, method, and realization from collapsing into each other when reasoning about AI architectures, retrieval pipelines, and research questions.

name: Bayesian reasoning
description: Framework for combining prior beliefs with new evidence to update confidence under uncertainty; for Barbara, less about formal probability and more about a disciplined habit of asking what assumptions a system carries, what evidence it weighs, and what would count as a meaningful update.

name: Sensemaking
description: Framework — drawn from organizational and information science — for how people construct coherent understanding from ambiguous, fragmented, or contested information; Barbara applies it to data legibility, human-AI collaboration, and the design of inspectable retrieval systems.

name: Visual attention
description: Cognitive process by which the brain prioritizes some information over others within a visual scene; Barbara's dissertation work on context-guided visual attention is the direct intellectual precursor to her current thinking about attention mechanisms and retrieval ranking in AI systems.

name: Attention as limited resource
description: Theoretical lens framing attention not as a spotlight that can illuminate anything but as a finite resource that must be allocated under constraints; informs Barbara's thinking about retrieval ranking, context window budgets, and where human-in-the-loop intervention earns its cost.

name: Mental models
description: Cognitive science concept that people understand and predict systems through internal representations; for Barbara, the operative design principle is that AI systems should align with the downstream user's mental model rather than override it.

name: Systems thinking
description: Approach of understanding behavior as emerging from interactions between parts rather than properties of any single part; underpins Barbara's instinct to reason about retrieval, evaluation, and deployment as a coupled pipeline rather than independent components.

name: Structured representations
description: Principle that information becomes both computationally useful and humanly trustworthy when given explicit, typed structure — graphs, ontologies, schemas — rather than left as unstructured text; the conceptual bridge between Barbara's cognitive science training and her knowledge engineering practice.

name: Representational Constraints
description: Cognitive science framework — closely related to Marr — that the form of an internal representation both enables and constrains what computations a system can perform; foundational to how Barbara thinks about knowledge graph schema choices and AI architecture trade-offs.

name: Data legibility
description: Barbara's central professional framing: the principle that data and AI outputs must be made legible — interpretable, contextualized, traceable — to both machines and the humans who depend on them; her self-identified contribution to the field.

name: Making meaning from messy data
description: Barbara's webpage tagline and the cross-cutting design challenge under which her cognitive science, consulting, and AI engineering work converge: producing coherent, trustworthy interpretation from ambiguous, fragmented, or unstructured inputs — with the explicit stance that the mess is where the meaning lives.

name: Evaluation as a design problem
description: Framing of evaluation not as a post-hoc validation step but as a first-class design discipline that shapes what gets built, what counts as ready, and what the system's contract with its users actually is; central to Barbara's LLM eval work and to the admin panel in her Digital Twin.

name: Grounded AI Systems
description: Paradigm in which AI outputs are explicitly traced to specific source data so the system's reasoning is inspectable and trustworthy; informs Barbara's work on RAG, citation-grounded retrieval, and the audit-trail design of her Digital Twin.

name: Human-centered design
description: Design philosophy that centers user needs, mental models, and lived workflows over technical elegance; Barbara's self-stated approach across consulting, cognitive science research, and AI engineering.

name: Pragmatic design
description: Design philosophy that prioritizes what works in practice and gets adopted by domain experts over solutions that are theoretically optimal but operationally fragile; the consulting-derived counterweight to research-purity instincts in Barbara's work.

name: Gap between technically correct and actually useful
description: The recurring tension between an AI system that performs well in evaluation and one that gets adopted in real workflows by real domain experts; Barbara treats this gap as the organizing problem of applied AI rather than a downstream usability concern.

name: Operational readiness
description: Consulting-derived framework — anchored in Inflective's Assert methodology — for assessing whether a system is genuinely ready for deployment across stakeholder alignment, role clarity, workflow fit, and real-world usability; the operational counterpart to evaluation-as-design.

name: Cognitive science as a design lens
description: Barbara's distinctive positioning: bringing cognitive science principles — attention, mental models, structured representations, contextual priors — to bear on the design of AI and retrieval systems; the throughline that connects her PhD to her contemporary engineering work.
alt_labels: cognitive science applied to AI systems

name: Model-building as perception
description: Cognitive science framing that perception is active model-construction from prior knowledge and incoming evidence, not passive registration of input; bridges Barbara's vision-science background to contemporary predictive coding and Bayesian inference in AI.
